title: practical english usage - Part 93
content:
The  infinitive of there is (there to be) can be used after for.

10 for    there      to   be It would make me very happy for her to lose the election.

(►   113.4  above).

However, it is more common for a structure with preparatory it to be used For her to lose the election would make me very happy.

fail now would be a disaster.

Thefor-structure can be the subject of a clause.

I explained enough for her to understand what was happening.

Do you think it's warm enough for the snow to melt?

There are too many people here for me to talk to all of them.

This is much  too heavy for you to lift.

A for-structure is often used after too and enough.

8  after    too    and   enough Did you mean for Jack to take the car?

She hates for people to feel sad.

I would like for you to stay as long as you want.

possible in British English.

with similar meanings can be used with a for-structure. This is not usually ɪn informal American English, like, hate, mean, intend and some other verbs It took twenty minutes for the smoke to clear.

When will it suit you for to call?

A  few other verbs can be used like this, e.g. suit and take (time).

Can you arrange for the gold to be delivered on Monday? (     .. .fbr-the-gold I can't wait for them to finish talking.

Anna asked for the designs to be ready by Friday.

+ object           +  infinitive.

pay, arrange) can often be used with for However, verbs which  are normally followed by for (e.g. ask, hope, wait, look, not I need you to help me. (     Ftuwdfof ymt to help me.) Tor-structures are not normally used as objects after verbs.

ask     for   . . . to   . . .

Infinitives, -ing    forms and Past Participles: Other Uses   Section 10 grammar *114   infinitives: other uses Rejected by society, he decided to become a monk.

Who’s the old man sitting in the corner?

Served with milk and sugar, it makes a delicious breakfast.

Not knowing what to do, I telephoned the police.

Most of the people invited to the reception were old friends.

There’s a woman crying her eyes out over there.

passive meanings.

Participles can combine with other words into participle clauses with active or 1  structures 115  participle       clauses      _ To put it another way, we’re spending more than we’re earning.

To sum  up, I think we all accept John’s proposal.

To tell the truth, I'm not sure what to do now.

To be honest, I think you’re making a mistake.

in speaking.

Some infinitive phrases are used to show the speaker’ s attitude or purpose 3  to   be   honest To hear her talk, you'd think she was made of money.

To see him  walk down the street, you’d never know he was blind.

To see them, you’d think they were married. But they only met yesterday.

similar expression.

impression. The  infinitive structure is usually followed by you’d think or a The  infinitives of see and hear can be used to explain the reason for a false 2  To   hear      her    talk,    you'd      think     ...

He spent four years studying, only to learn that there were no jobs.

At last we got to Amy ’s place, only to discover that she was away.

The  idea of surprise or disappointment can be emphasised by using only.

I arrived home to find that the house had been broken into.

end of a journey or task.

Infinitive clauses can be used to say what somebody found out or learnt at the 1  I arrived        home       to   find    ...

114 infinitives:          other      uses grammar *115  participle clauses Wrapped in red and gold gift paper, I delivered the parcel to my girlfriend.

with snow. (This could sound as if the mountains were looking out of Looking out of the window of our hotel room, the mountains were covered subject from the main clause.

(often called ’ dangling participles'), where an adverb clause has a different It is often considered incorrect to make sentences with misrelated participles subject of explaining.) My wife had a talk with Sophie, explaining the problem. (My wife is the of the main clause in a sentence.

Normally the subject of an adverbial participle clause is the same as the subject the    mountains           . ..
